headline: Global hunger declines for third year as 645 million still struggle
meta_description: Global hunger fell to 7.8% in 2025, but Africa now hosts the most hungry people. Progress remains fragile and uneven across regions.
summary_short: For the third consecutive year, global hunger declined—but the gains hide a troubling shift: Africa now has more hungry people than any other continent.
body:
For the third consecutive year, global hunger has declined, marking real progress toward ending one of humanity's most stubborn crises. In 2025, approximately 645 million people—7.8 per cent of the global population—faced hunger, down from 8.1 per cent in 2024 and 8.6 per cent in 2022. That represents nearly 14 million fewer people than the previous year and 43 million fewer than three years ago. Yet this hopeful trend masks a troubling geographic shift revealed in the latest State of Food Security and Nutrition in the World report issued by five United Nations agencies: Africa now hosts the largest number of hungry people in absolute terms for the first time, with 309 million facing hunger compared to 292 million in Asia.

The uneven recovery reflects deeper structural inequalities. While Asia, Latin America and the Caribbean have recorded steady gains, Africa's hunger rate—though it decreased slightly from 20.3 per cent to 20.0 per cent—affects more people because of rapid population growth. Beyond those without enough food, the picture grows starker: roughly 2.1 billion people globally experienced moderate or severe food insecurity in 2025, forced to compromise on what or how much they ate. In Africa, this affects more than half the population at 56.6 per cent.

The fragility of these gains cannot be overstated. Conflict, climate extremes, and reduced development assistance threaten to reverse progress. According to FAO Chief Economist Máximo Torero, depending on how long geopolitical crises affect food inflation through energy and fertilizer prices, between 510 and 520 million people could still face hunger by 2030. Meanwhile, a healthy diet remains unaffordable for 2.69 billion people worldwide, with 66.6 per cent of Africa's population unable to afford one. The UN agencies call for targeted investments in infrastructure and systemic reforms—not just lower prices, but a redesigned food system that puts nutritious meals within reach for nearly one in three people still locked out by cost.
quick_facts: 645 million people faced hunger in 2025, down 14 million from 2024
Africa now has most hungry people: 309 million (vs. 292 million in Asia)
2.1 billion people experienced moderate or severe food insecurity globally
66.6% of Africa's population cannot afford a healthy diet
Between 510–520 million may face hunger by 2030 under current trends
